entry.title: I am a consultant. Where can I start learning how the basic procurement process works?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Introducing Procurement Processes learning journey.


Overview
This course is a beginner course designed to give consultants who are completely new to the procurement area an overview of how the basic procurement process generally works. These could, for example, be consultants who have previously worked in other areas and are now being used in the procurement process. They should then be able to distinguish the processes between SAP S/4 HANA and SAP Ariba and be able to identify where there is potential for integration between the two solutions.

Learning objectives
At the end of this course, the learner will be able to:

  • Demonstrate the fundamental procurement principles, practices, and processes, enabling them to navigate the procurement lifecycle effectively.
  • Develop the skills necessary to analyze procurement requirements, select appropriate procurement methods, and manage supplier relationships efficiently within diverse organizational contexts.

Pass all the quizzes and receive a digital badge

Check the related Live Sessions and engage and learn with SAP experts and peers.

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area.

 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: How to check the TLS Version
entry.content:
How can we verify the current TLS version? Is a version update required?

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to check the TLS Version
entry.content:
To check the SSL version being used you can use a tool like SSL Labs' SSL Test. Here’s how you can do it:

  1. Go to the SSL Labs SSL Test tool. (https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/)
  2. Enter the URL into the provided field.
  3. The tool will analyze and provide detailed information about the supported SSL versions and cipher suites.


Cipher Suites used are only TLS: 1.2 and 1.3, there is no lower version TLS used by default in SAP Commerce Cloud. For more information, see Trusted TLS/SSL Ciphers.

entry.title: Re: ABAP Core Development Tools 3.42.0 vs ABAP Core Devel...
entry.content:
Dear Ingo,

First of all thank you for your guidance. You were right, the issue was at my side. I am now describing what I have done in case it can help anyone else.

First of all, apologies for a subject misleading

The wizard in ABAP Core Development Tools 3.46.0 is different than the ABAP Core Development Tools 3.42.0 but it has nothing to do with the root-cause.

The important here is the error messaged that I received before solving the issue:

"

Failed to get service instance URL info for

 https: // XXXXX.abap.us10.hana.ondemand.com: java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException: org.apache.hc.client5.http.ClientProtocolException: CONNECT refused by proxy: HTTP/1.1 407 Proxy"

The important part here is the last 2 words: 407 which means authorization and proxy. In other words your proxy is blocking the access to the SAP BTP trial account

Standard solution would be to go to Eclipse > Menu > Window > Preferences > General > Network connections and write the IP of your proxy and the password.

I am trying to connect with my company notebook which has PROXY centrally managed, so I need to create a ticket at my company and they solve it. You can close now this question and mark it as solved.

entry.title: Loading Time by Crane to be displayed on Order confirmation and Delivery confirmation (outbound)
entry.content:
Dear All,

I have one requirement received as below:

Customer wants loading done by Crane for material  to be included exactly with Time and agreed Rate on delivery note copy and order confirmation.

Currently on delivery note it is appeared as below:

Material No                      QTY Unit      Unit price     Unit       Net price USD
AB23000BW                    10 TO(ton)       35              1 TO      350
special Crane of 40M

Customer suggests  now as below. The query is how can we achieve this technically without ABAP code :

The suggestion would now be that the recorded tonnage is multiplied by a fixed value of 10 minutes and displayed either as text or as an additional item number, e.g.: “The included unloading time is XX minutes” or as a separate item number “Included unloading time".

Item number also they need to be appeared automatically as soon as they select "Crane loading" in Delivery creation.

Thank you for the help.

Regards

SAPSCM

entry.title: Re: ME21N CHECK ON WARNING RED-LIGHTS NEEDS TO STOP THE P...
entry.content:
First correct your implementing class of ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST

  • Use provided macros to raise error message
  • In case of error invalidate the item in error

Sample for error handling

 

 

          mmpur_metafield mmmfd_cust_01.
          MESSAGE e083(me) WITH text-004 space INTO lv_dummy.
          mmpur_message_forced sy-msgty sy-msgid sy-msgno
                               sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4.
          CALL METHOD im_item->invalidate( ).

 

 

There's another BAdI for disabling hold/parking options: ME_HOLD_PO

  • But first try disabling faulty elements, especially in any CHECK method loop
  • You don't want an angry user to lose his 1000 items input cause one item is in error, just before week-end?

entry.title: Inner join with AND , OR and LIKE in where clause select query
entry.content:
Hi Gurus,

               When I am using the multiple OR clauses in where condition for individual tables it is working fine but when using the multiple OR  clauses with inner join in where clause it is only considering the first condition. 

The following is the code which helps you understand better.

 

    if lt_bsad is not initial.
      select rbukrs,
             gjahr,
             belnr,
             wsl,
             augbl,
             gkont from acdoca into table @LT_acdoca
        for all entries in @LT_bsad where rbukrs = @LT_bsad-bukrs and
                                          gjahr  = @LT_bsad-gjahr and
                                          augbl  = @LT_bsad-augbl and
                                          rldnr  = '0L'.

    endif.

    if lt_acdoca is not initial.
      select spras,
             saknr,
             txt50 from skat into table @DATA(lt_skat)
        for all entries in @LT_acdoca where saknr = @LT_acdoca-gkont and
                                            txt50 like '%TDS%' or
                                            txt50 like '%CASH DISCOUNT%' or
                                            txt50 like '%GBI%' or
                                            txt50 like '%CUF%' and
                                            spras = 'E'.
    endif.

 The above code is picking 6 records each in lt_acdoca and lt_skat which is absolutely correct. 
But I want to get the inner join of both acdoca and skat so that I can pick the acdoca-wsl from acdoca to pass it in final internal table based on txt50 as shown above.
The below is the select query with inner join of the above two tables.

    select acdoca~rbukrs,
           acdoca~gjahr,
           acdoca~belnr,
           acdoca~wsl,
           acdoca~augbl,
           acdoca~gkont,
           skat~spras,
           skat~saknr,
           skat~txt50
         from acdoca as acdoca inner join skat as skat
     on skat~saknr = acdoca~gkont
      into table @DATA(lt_acdoca_skat)
    for all entries in @LT_bsad where  acdoca~rbukrs = @LT_bsad-bukrs and
                                        acdoca~gjahr  = @LT_bsad-gjahr and
                                        acdoca~augbl  = @LT_bsad-augbl and
                                        acdoca~rldnr  = '0L' and
                                        skat~spras = 'E' and
                                        ( skat~txt50 like '%TDS%' or
                                          skat~txt50 like '%CASH DISCOUNT%' 
                                                                     or
                                         skat~txt50 like '%GBI%' or
                                          skat~txt50 like '%CUF%'
                                         ).
*

Now it is giving the result with 'TDS' only . But I want the lt_acdoca_skat with 'GBI' , 'CUF', 'CASH DISCOUNT' also.

The below is the image of 6 records in lt_skat

The below is the one record with the TDS. So it is taking the first condition of TDS and skipping the others.

I want the lt_acdoca_skat with all 6 records of lt_acdoca and lt_skat .

How can I achieve it  ?

entry.title: Re: Issue with Guest User Provisioning to SAP IAS
entry.content:
Hi Ari

If you have enabled "create shadows users during logon" for the IDP named "IAS" i guess that what you see there on those "weird" users is just the automatic creation happending once a guest logins for the first time. The username might come from the "sub" or some of the claims from openID that has such format. The emails "xxxx@user.from.sap.custom.cf" are created when BTP is not able to resolve the mail attribute from IDP.

If you have configured the IDP named "IAS" with the standard option "establish trust" I'd have a look at the application created in IAS named "SAP BTP Subaccount xxxxx" and check the attribute mappings to make sure the attributes you're using exist for the Guest users.

entry.title: Re: ABAP Units test environment class CL_FUNCTION_TEST_ENVIRONMENT/If_FUNCTION_TEST_ENVIRONMENT does
entry.content:
The basic idea is to replace all external calls like the call of a Function module by mocks. So the productive code will call a class which implements the BAPI call in a method via an interface and the unit test calls a mock class sharing the same interface, does nothing, but returning the expected / desired value. Maybe a bit old but in my opinion still worth. 
Especially if you do not have the testdoubles in your system. 
Writing Testable Code for ABAP (sap.com)

entry.title: Re: Report on Work Location in Time Posting
entry.content:
Hi Rao,

The API https://api.sap.com/api/API_MANAGE_WORKFORCE_TIMESHEET/path/get_TimeSheetEntryCollection gives you the worklocation code in the timesheet. Likewise, the CDS view I_TimeSheetRecord provides you the work location code. You can build your own custom report via key user extensibility with this CDS view.

Regards,

Dennis

entry.title: Re: Two Major News with SUM 2.0 SP 17
entry.content:
Hi Borris,

We are working with DMOVE2S4 approach for conversion to S/4HANA. 

I got a SAP ppt where it is showing that AAS must be installed on Migration Server pointing to source ECC system. I tried to simulate same approach but it is not working because it is not getting details of ASCS instance which is but obvious because /sapmnt is not shared between source ECC system and new server ( which is similar to migration server) so it will not detect presence of ASCS. However, it is connecting to DB of source ECC system and stopping/starting successfully. I have tried multiple SAP notes with no success. 

Your blog is mentioning that AAS must be installed on the HANA DB server pointing to source ECC system. 

Now question is SAP is not going to give us access on HANA DB server but will give full access on Migration Server. Then in that case what is the proposed solution in this situation. 

We are in between and loosing the timeline of the project. Request your quick response. 

Best Regards,

Sandeep

entry.title: AI@FRE (PART 4) - Guardrail Enterprise AI Adoption – AI Architecture Guardrails
entry.content:
### AI@FRE Blog Series:

AI@FRE (Part 1) - Enabling Enterprise AI Adoption through the BTP FRE Engagement Model

AI@FRE (Part 2) - Accelerating Business AI Transformation with the FRE Value Framework

AI@FRE (Part 3) – Guardrails for Enterprise AI Adoption

AI@FRE (PART 4) - Guardrail Enterprise AI Adoption – AI Architecture Guardrails 

###

Scaling enterprise AI is no longer optional; it's essential for organizations looking to remain competitive. However, achieving this requires robust architecture and tool standardization to enable scalable, secure, and efficient AI deployment. Standardizing key areas in an organization, from security to automation, digital experiences, core, data, and AI architecture, sets a strong foundation for streamlined processes, enhanced agility, and a simplified landscape. 

The Importance of Standardizing Architecture and Tools for AI at Scale 

Building a scalable AI infrastructure hinges on standardizing architecture and tools, which enables enterprises to measure key outcomes like: 

  • Business Agility & Flexibility: Achieve XX-month reduction in time-to-market for new AI initiatives. 
  • Execution Speed: Drive XX-day reduction in project execution. 
  • Simplified Landscape: Foster streamlined architecture that minimizes redundancies and supports easy upgrades and scalability. 

Each area of standardization brings unique benefits and helps ensure consistent integration across SAP solutions, as we’ll explore below. 

 

  1. Standardize Security Architecture 

With AI adoption, ensuring security at every layer of the architecture is critical. A standardized security architecture enables cohesive, scalable, and adaptive access management. 

  • User Access Management (IPS): Enforces access control, minimizing security risks by managing permissions and restricting access to sensitive data. 
  • Single Sign-On (IAS): Unifies login experiences across SAP solutions, enabling employees to access tools seamlessly. 
  • Automated, Adaptive Access Management: Automates access protocols to ensure compliance, adaptive access control, and lifecycle management. 
  • Event-Driven Identity Lifecycle Management: Automates identity management processes in response to organizational events, streamlining updates to user access and permissions. 

Business Benefits: 

  • Unified Access: Simplifies security management by centralizing access across SAP environments. 
  • Secure Remote Work: Provides reliable access for remote employees without compromising security. 
  • Enhanced Employee Experience: Eliminates repetitive logins, allowing employees to focus on tasks. 
  • Productivity and Collaboration: Reduces friction in accessing necessary tools, fostering productivity. 
  • Digital Transformation: Lays the security groundwork for digital transformation efforts across the organization. 

 

Enabling of single-sign-on and identity provisioning throughout your application landscape 

 

  1. Standardize Enterprise Automation 

Automating workflows and processes is essential for scaling AI-driven enterprise operations. Standardized automation supports consistent, efficient, and error-free processes. De Agostini Publishing S.p.A leveraged SAP BTP to automate vendor invoice management, enhancing accuracy, efficiency, and data reliability across new markets. Another example is from Colsubsidio’s SAP BTP implementation, which enables them efficient, independent analytics for strategic, data-driven decision-making. 

  • Automate Visually: Utilize intuitive, drag-and-drop interfaces and AI to simplify automation. 
  • Integrate Seamlessly: Speed up development with pre-built workflows, bots, and connectors for both SAP and non-SAP systems. 
  • Collaborate Securely: Empower cross-functional teams with secure, agile development tools. 

Automation Examples: 

  • Generative AI for Interactive Modeling: Supports modeling through natural language inputs with proactive validation. 
  • Document Information Extraction: Automates data capture from structured documents like invoices. 
  • Action Process AI (SAP Signavio): Combines large process models with SAP expertise to improve accuracy and effectiveness. 

Key Benefits: 

  • Reduced manual effort and error rates. 
  • Increased speed and quality of automation. 
  • Empowered cross-functional teams with collaboration capabilities. 

 

Process AI at SAP Signavio 

 

  1. Standardize Digital Experience Architecture for Conversational Experience 

To create a seamless digital experience, the architecture must support modularity and scalability, allowing for a unified user interface across devices and applications. 

  • Conversational UX: Enable conversational interfaces across desktop and mobile, enhancing the user experience with intuitive, end-to-end interactions. 
  • SAP Start for Cloud Solutions: A modular homepage offers a single point of entry with pre-configured access to cloud solutions like SAP SuccessFactors and S/4HANA Cloud. 
  • Joule in SAP Applications: SAP’s digital assistant, Joule, is embedded in applications to enhance user experience, starting with SAP Start and SAP SuccessFactors. 

AI-powered Talent Intelligence: 

  • Talent Optimization: Use AI to generate job descriptions and interview questions tailored to organizational needs, streamlining HR processes. 
  • Skills-Based Matching: Empower skills-based organizations with talent intelligence to identify and promote internal talent efficiently. 

Standardize UX Architecture – The SAP UX Story 

 

  1. Standardize Core - Building a Lean Core 

A lean core structure is essential to keep AI-enabled systems agile and cloud-compliant, enhancing scalability and reducing potential disruptions. 

  • Avoid Custom Extensions: Minimize customizations to ensure compatibility with SAP’s upgrade cycles. 
  • Separate Extensions with APIs: Ensure extensions do not interfere with upgrades by leveraging APIs. 
  • Cloud-Compliant Extensions: Develop with cloud compatibility in mind, aligning with a three-tier architectural model. 

Business Impact: 

  • Smooth upgrades, minimal disruptions. 
  • Scalable, cloud-ready architecture. 
  • Enhanced reliability in deployments. 

 

  1. Standardize Data Architecture 

Data architecture standardization is vital for ensuring seamless data sharing, comprehensive analytics, and powerful AI model training across the enterprise. 

  • Business Data Fabric: An architecture that delivers contextually rich data to every user, preserving business context and logic. 
  • Intelligent Data Applications: Enable developers to create apps that are AI-driven, context-aware, and seamlessly connected to core business data. 
  • Extended Planning and Analysis: Unify planning functions for finance, HR, and operations within a single, data-rich ecosystem. 
  • Data Sharing & Products: Facilitate data-sharing with data products that provide both abstraction and usability layers without introducing new artifacts. 

Example Use Case: 

  • Business Decision Simulator: Simulate complex scenarios to predict potential disruptions and make data-informed adjustments. 

Core Benefits: 

  • Holistic data access across departments. 
  • Stronger alignment between operational and strategic planning. 
  • Enhanced data-driven decision-making. 

 

  1. Standardize AI Architecture 

The AI architecture must support diverse AI capabilities, from natural language processing to generative AI, ensuring seamless integration and adaptability across SAP applications. 

  • Joule in All Apps: SAP’s digital assistant Joule, embedded in SAP applications, offers users insights and support while automating routine tasks. It allows users to directly access services like SAP SuccessFactors and S/4HANA Cloud. 
  • Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core: The GenAI Hub provides a central location for generative AI models, enabling SAP applications to draw upon AI capabilities like text generation, summarization, and process modeling in real time. 
  • SAP-Hosted Foundation Models: By hosting foundational AI models, SAP AI Core offers scalability and reliability, reducing dependency on external AI solutions and ensuring secure data handling. 

Benefits: 

  • Scalability: Supports consistent deployment across all SAP applications, reducing the need for fragmented, custom AI solutions. 
  • Data Security: Centralizes model hosting within SAP’s secure environment. 
  • Enhanced Process Intelligence: Infuses advanced process and data understanding across SAP systems, allowing companies to optimize AI-driven workflows effectively. 

Conclusion 

Standardizing architecture and tools across these six key areas creates a solid foundation for scalable, secure, and efficient AI adoption in enterprise settings. This standardization empowers organizations to move faster, adapt more readily to changes, and achieve a simplified and agile landscape that supports continued innovation and growth. 

 

Curious on a structured customer engagement model to set up standardized Cloud Architecture for Business AI Adoption? Connect with our Global BTP Customer Engagement Team at BTPFRE@sap.com.

entry.title: DVM Report
entry.content:
Dear team,

 

We are configuring DVM report in our Production system. We are reaching out to inquire about the process for generating a Data Volume Management (DVM) report within the SAP environment.

Specifically, I would like to understand if there is an option to access or generate this report at no additional cost, as part of the standard offerings or through any available free tools or services.

 

Could you please provide information on:

 

1. The steps or procedures for generating the DVM report within SAP.
2. Any free resources or tools that might allow us to generate this report without incurring additional charges.
3. Any potential limitations or prerequisites for accessing the report without fees.
4. Proceess and steps of how to generate DVM reports

Your guidance on how to proceed would be greatly appreciated. Please let me know if you require any further details from my end.

Thank you for your assistance, and I look forward to your response.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: DVM Report
entry.content:
There are several ways to generate a Data Volume Management (DVM) report for an SAP system. Here are some common methods:

1. SAP Solution Manager: SAP Solution Manager provides various tools and functionalities to manage and monitor SAP systems. It includes the DVM Work Center, which allows you to generate DVM reports. You can access the DVM Work Center through the SAP Solution Manager Launchpad and use the provided templates to create reports.

2. SAP Data Volume Management Cockpit: The Data Volume Management Cockpit is a tool specifically designed for managing data volume in SAP systems. It provides a graphical interface to analyze and optimize data volume. You can generate DVM reports by selecting the relevant data objects, specifying the reporting period, and running the analysis.

3. SAP EarlyWatch Alert: The SAP EarlyWatch Alert service provides proactive monitoring and analysis of SAP systems. It includes a DVM component that analyzes data volume trends and provides recommendations for optimization. You can request an EarlyWatch Alert report from SAP Support, which will include DVM-related information.

4. Custom Reports: You can develop custom reports using SAP tools like ABAP (Advanced Business Application Programming) or SAP BusinessObjects. These reports can extract data from SAP tables and provide insights into data volume management. Custom reports can be tailored to specific requirements and provide more flexibility in terms of data selection and analysis.

 

It is important to note that the availability and functionality of DVM reporting may vary depending on the SAP system version and the specific tools and licenses in use.

If your environment meets the prerequisites below, I can also recommend to use the DVM App. Please refer to the following KBA: 2716655 - How To Use the Data Volume Management APP - Step by Step guide

  • SAP Solution Manager 7.2 SP3+ 
  • Managed System Prerequisites:  ST-PI 740 SP07
                                                   ST-A/PI 01S SP03
  • Prerequisites:
        - A TMW RFC destination is required, pointing to the client in the system to be analyzed.
        - The user requires DVM administration rights in the SAP Solution Manager system.
  • Supported Managed System: SAP S/4 HANA , SAP Business Suite on HANA ( only EHP7+ for SAP ERP 6.0 for the moment ), SAP BW/4 HANA 

With the Data Volume Management APP, there is no ST14 analysis anymore, instead a job is executed which triggers report RAGS_DVM_CQC_CONTROL to run in your SAP Solution Manager system. RAGS_DVM_CQC_CONTROL subsequently triggers a second job, AGS_DVM_CQC_ANALYSIS, in your managed system, which performs an analysis in respect of Data Volume Management in the managed system. After the analysis is completed, the control job in the SAP Solution Manager system fetches the result from the managed system and sends this result to the SAP Support Backbone. Once the data is successfully sent to the SAP support backbone, the temporarily created analysis data will be removed from the system.

After the analysis is finished, you can find the analysis result in the Data Volume Management APP in SAP For Me portal: Data Volume Management - SAP for Me. If your current user has an email address maintained, you can decide to receive an e-mail notification with the URL link to the SAP For Me portal where you can view the final result in the Data Volume Management APP.

entry.title: The AI Adoption Journey with SAP Fieldglass
entry.content:
There has been a ton of excitement and talk about AI with SAP from existing functionality to newer developments on the horizons.

Personally, I've heard a lot about AI, but I have yet to bridge the gap fully in understanding what it means for what we do here at SAP!

I want to lock in and understand the changes with SAP software and its AI capabilities from the basics, starting with what AI is, practical use cases, and how SAP has used it to further innovation and development.

The term "AI" or Artificial Intelligence is quickly becoming a central focus in almost every area of development with technology available to us as consumers. The definition for AI is so vague that it covers so many possibilities. It becomes difficult to nail down exactly why it matters and how it can impact what we want to work on in the immediate future. 

The main purpose for AI is found in its capacity to perform tasks that, historically, only people could perform. These tasks generally involve anything that involves reasoning, decision-making, problem solving. 


AI sounds like its constantly in development, because it is! But its usage is far more accessible than it sounds, especially with a type of AI called "Machine Learning". 

Machine Learning sounds a bit intimidating, but it basically gives technology the ability to predict outcomes and classify information. 

In fact, you've likely already interacted with different forms of machine learning AI! If you have ever:

  • Come across recommendations based on your tastes in media (Spotify playlist recommendations, "For You" sections on any movie streaming services)
  • Used the speech to text feature on your cell phone, or any sort of transcription/translation features in teams
  • Gotten any alerts from your banking app about flagging or monitoring transaction activity

All of these things that we interact with daily, are all possible because of machine learning AI. 

As AI changes the way we experience technology, how can we leverage it to positively impact our business? More specifically, how can AI best benefit our SAP Fieldglass customers? 

Join me in a deep dive of the existing AI and Machine Learning features to understand the features and functionality and explore up-and-coming innovations with SAP AI in our new series The AI Adoption Journey!

entry.title: Monitor Supplier Confirmations (F2359)
entry.content:
Hello SAP,
Business  planning into activate supplier  confirmation senario  for this do we require EDI for both systems  or we can convert  vendor email confirmation into SAP order confirmation  via CPI.
Please guide us.
 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Monitor Supplier Confirmations (F2359)
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

1. EDI-Based Supplier Confirmation (Both Systems Use EDI)

 

How it works:

  • The supplier sends an order confirmation through EDI (using formats like IDoc or EDIFACT).
  • Your SAP system automatically receives and processes this confirmation, creating an order in SAP without manual entry.

Using SAP CPI for Email-Based Confirmations (Without EDI)

If your supplier doesn't use EDI, you can use SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) to handle email-based order confirmations.

How it works:

  • The supplier sends an order confirmation by email (e.g., as a PDF or Excel file).
  • SAP CPI extracts the data from the email (like order number, quantities, etc.) and automatically creates an order confirmation in SAP.

Kindly refer to below help portal:

Receiving Vendor Confirmations via EDI

 

Thank you

SAP SUPPORT

entry.title: Replication Flows Blog Series Part 8 – Confluent as a Replication Source
entry.content:
This blog is part of a blog series from SAP Datasphere product management with the focus on the Replication Flow capabilities in SAP Datasphere:  

Replication Flow Blog Series Part 1 – Overview | SAP Blogs  

Replication Flow Blog Series Part 2 – Premium Outbound Integration | SAP Blogs  

Replication Flows Blog Series Part 3 – Integration with Kafka  

Replication Flows Blog Series Part 4 – Sizing   

Replication Flows Blog Series Part 5 – Integration between SAP Datasphere and Databricks  

Replication Flows Blog Series Part 6 – Confluent as a Replication Target

Replication Flows Blog Series Part 7 – Performance

Replication Flows Blog Series Part 8 - Confluent as a Replication Source

Data Integration is an essential topic in a Business Data Fabric like SAP Datasphere. Replication Flow is the cornerstone to fuel SAP Datasphere with data, especially from SAP ABAP sources. There is also a big need to move data from third party sources into SAP Datasphere to succeed certain use cases. 

In this part of the Replication Flow Blog series, we focus on the usage of Confluent as a source in Replication Flows. We will explain in detail the new capabilities that have been introduced with SAP Datasphere release 2024.23. The content of this blog is structured as follows.

  1. Introduction
  2. Sample Scenario & Confluent specific Setup
  3. Configuration options for Replication Flows
  4. Replication Flow Runtime Specifics
  5. Scenarios

 

1. Introduction

The purpose of the additional features that are described in this Blog, is to provide tailor-made integration with our dedicated partner Confluent’s Apache Kafka®-based data streaming platform—both fully managed Confluent Cloud and self-managed Confluent Platform. More specifically, we now provide the possibility to consume Kafka messages from Confluent, respectively, in SAP Datasphere. The content of this blog does not apply to the generic Kafka integration in SAP Datasphere.

The Confluent integration described in this blog is only usable in SAP Datasphere Replication Flows.

For the examples and step-by-step instructions in the blog, we assume that a properly configured SAP Datasphere tenant and a Confluent Cloud cluster are available. In addition, we assume that the reader is familiar with the basic concepts around Replication Flows and Connection Management in SAP Datasphere as well as with the Kafka data streaming capabilities of Confluent. For a guide on how to setup a connection to Confluent Cloud via the SAP Datasphere Connection Management, we refer the reader to Part 6 of this blog series. If we want to refer/link to Confluent specific material, for the sake of consistency we will always use the Confluent Cloud documentation as well as related material.

 

2. Overview & Sample Scenario

In order to explain the various aspects of the consumption capabilities in SAP Datasphere Replication Flows for Confluent Kafka data we use the following sample setup. It is solely intended to explain the new functionalities and does not claim to fully or partially represent real scenarios. We assume that we have employee data that is stored in a Kafka topic Demo_Topic consisting of 6 partitions as illustrated in the following figure.

Figure 1 - Kafka Messages with employee data

The employee Kafka messages comply with the following JSON schema whose subject is named Demo_Topic-value and is available in the default schema registry context.

 

{
  "$id": "http://example.com/myURI.schema.json",
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "additionalProperties": false,
  "description": "Sample schema",
  "properties": {
    "history": {
      "items": [
        {
          "properties": {
            "end": {
              "type": "string"
            },
            "position": {
              "type": "string"
            },
            "start": {
              "type": "string"
            }
          },
          "required": [
            "position",
            "start",
            "end"
          ],
          "type": "object"
        }
      ],
      "type": "array"
    },
    "id": {
      "type": "integer"
    },
    "personal": {
      "properties": {
        "email": {
          "type": "string"
        },
        "firstName": {
          "type": "string"
        },
        "secondName": {
          "type": "string"
        }
      },
      "required": [
        "firstName",
        "secondName",
        "email"
      ],
      "type": "object"
    },
    "position": {
      "type": "string"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "id",
    "personal",
    "position"
  ],
  "title": "SampleRecord",
  "type": "object"
}

 

We will also consider AVRO schemas in this blog.

Confluent Schema Registry is a prerequisite when using a Confluent system as a source in Replication Flows. Additionally, the messages must contain the schema ID in the bytes that follow the magic byte at the beginning of the message. How schemas are used during the replication process is described in the next section.

Replication Flows itself can consume topics with Kafka messages that comply with schemas of type AVRO or JSON.

 

3. Configuration Options for Replication Flows

We now run through the creation process of a Replication Flow. In parallel we will describe the different configuration options that are available during design time of a Replication Flow in case a Confluent system is selected as the source. For this blog, we use SAP Datasphere as the replication target. Of course, in a real-world scenario, any system that is supported by Replication Flows as a replication target can be used. As mentioned in the first section of this blog, we assume that a connection to the source Confluent system has been set up in the Connection Management. In our case, this connection is named CONFLUENT_DEMO.

After we have entered the Replication Flow creation screen via the  , we select CONFLUENT_DEMO as the source connection. A Confluent Schema Context always serves as a Replication Flow Source Container. The default schema context, that we also use in our example, is always denoted with a dot (.). But the Replication Flow Source Container selection screen lists all available schema contexts. In the Replication Flow Source Object selection screen we can choose one or several Kafka topics that are available in the Confluent Cloud cluster. In our example we only select Demo_Topic. Finally, we select the SAP Datasphere local repository as the replication target.

Figure 2 - Replication Flow with Confluent as Source

Although there is no direct relation between Confluent schema contexts and Kafka topics, like it is the case for schemas and tables in classical relational database systems, the two Confluent artifacts are used to derive a structure definition of the replication target (e.g. a HANA table schema in our example). We provide the details below.

As highlighted in Figure 2, for each replication object, in the Object Properties Panel of the Replication Flow screen there are the four standard sections General, Projections, Settings and Target Columns as well as two confluent specific sections Confluent: Source Settings and Confluent: Source Schema Settings. The General and Target Columns section do not require additional explanation.

Settings

It is possible to decide whether Delta Capture Columns are added to the replication target as well as whether the replication target is truncated before the replication starts. For Confluent Kafka sources, only the load type Initial and Delta is supported. Which parts of a topic are actually replicated, depends on the Starting Point that can be configured in the section Confluent: Source Settings (see below).

Figure 3 - General Replication Task Settings

Projections

For each Kafka topic that is added as a source in a Replication Flow there is a projection automatically generated. One can do the usual configurations via the Projection screens.

Figure 4 - Projection Settings

The projection is created automatically due to the way how the Kafka message body of a Kafka message is flattened into a table format. We will provide the details later in the next chapter about Replication Flow Runtime Specifics.

Confluent: Source Schema Settings

The section provides an overview of the Confluent schema that is used to deserialize the Kafka messages of the selected Kafka topic. It also provides a possibility to configure/change the schema that is supposed to be used.

Figure 5 - Source Schema Settings

During the workflow showcased in Figure 2 an initial proposal for the schema to be used, is derived according to the following rules:

  • The latest message is read from the topic, the schema id is extracted to obtain the schema + subject + version from the schema registry given the specified context
  • If there is no message in the Kafka topic, the schema registry (considering the context) is checked for a schema following the topic-name subject naming strategy. If no schema definition is found, no value for the schema is set.
  • If the detected schema is of type PROTOBUF no value is set for the schema.
  • Always the latest version of a detected schema is used

The source Confluent schema, which is a JSON/AVRO nested structure, is then translated into a flat representation according to certain rules (see chapter Replication Flow Runtime Specifics).

In Figure 5 the main area of the schema configuration screen shows the resulting flat representation of the Kafka schema (i.e. viewable via the button Flat) as well as a mapping of the columns of the flat representation to the original nested schema (i.e. viewable via the button Nested). The nested schema uses  to specify the data types of the different properties/fields. The Apache Arrow types are only mentioned for the sake of completeness since they are used internally for type mapping.

On the Properties panel on the left side a Confluent schema definition can be selected via Subject Name and a Schema Version dropdown menus.

In addition, one can choose to include/exclude a Technical Key in the target schema. If set, an additional column __message_id of type String(39) is added that constitutes a primary key of the target table derived from the Kafka message in the following way. The value for that column of a row originating from a Kafka message from partition p and offset o is “<p>-<o>”.

Confluent: Source Settings

The section contains configuration options to fine tune the topic consumption behaviour.

Figure 6 - Confluent: Source Settings

The following table lists explains the different configuration options that are shown in Figure 4 above.

Configuration

Value(s)

Explanation

Consume Other Schema Versions

True/False

If set to True, a message (M) that is retrieved and which has a different schema version as opposed to what is configured in the Confluent: Source Schema Settings is tried to be mapped to the configured schema (S) according to the following rules.

  • Fail if the message has a different serialization type than the schema S specifies
  • Columns in the message M that are not present in schema S are ignored
  • Columns that are not present in M but in S are set to NULL in the inferred row. Fail, if the column is part of the primary key.
  • Fail if a column is present in both M and S but has incompatible types.

The Ignore Schema Missmatch configuration allows to configure the behaviour of the replication in case the schema mapping fails.

If set to False, the replication partition will fail with an error stating that the schemas do not match. The subject is always required to be identical to the one specified via the Confluent Source Schema Settings configuration.

Remark:
Schema Compatibility Features of Confluent Schema Registry are not supported.

Ignore Schema Missmatch

True/False

This option is only available if Consume Other Schema is set to True.

If set to True, messages that do not map to the configured schema (see Consume Other Schema Versions) are ignored and the replication continues.

If set to False, the replication partition will fail if a received message with a different schema cannot be mapped to the configured schema.

Fail on Data Truncation

True/False

If set to True, a replication partition fails if a source string field is encountered that does not fit into a string of at most 5000 chars. Also applies to binary values exceeding a length of 5000 bytes.

If set to False, string fields larger than 5000 characters are automatically truncated and written into the target column. The replication continues.

Starting Point

Read from earliest offset / Read from latest offset

Read from earliest offset
The topics are read from the oldest available partition offsets

Read from latest offset
Read only messages newer than the ones present when the replication starts the delta mode phase

 

4. Replication Flow Run specifics

In this section we will have a closer look on certain behaviour during a replication run. We start with some generic bullet points.

  • During the replication, all Kafka partitions of the specified source topic are replicated into   target. Kafka partitions that are added over time to the source topic are also replicated after some time.
  • Only the content of the Kafka message body is considered for the replication process. Kafka message keys are ignored.
  • Consumption of Kafka messages is always working via the READ_COMMITTED isolation level.   

We now provide details regarding the flattening of Kafka source messages.

Message Flattening & Type Mapping

As indicated in the last chapter there is a flattening process involved when setting up and running a Replication Flow. The Confluent schema needs to be flattened into a proper target schema and the content of the Kafka messages need to be translated into the flat target schema. The flattening process for the schema is a two step process. From a high level perspective the flow looks as follows.

Figure 7 - Flattening Process

Let’s take our example topic with the corresponding simplified JSON schema. In the figure below we also added a sample message body and included a possible AVRO schema definition of the JSON schema.

Figure 8 - Sample Schema & Message

In our example the first step is illustrated in the following figure.

Figure 9 - Generating a flat representation of a nested schema

The following rules are applied to flatten the JSON/AVRO schema into the flat Source Schema.

  • JSON/AVRO Arrays and AVRO Maps are omitted (support for arrays and maps is planned in a subsequent release in 2025)
  • AVRO unions are only supported if and only if there are exactly two types in the union, one of them is the null type and the second type is a supported one.
  • Nested properties are flattened using a naming convention like a simplified JSON path notation.

Using the above sample message, we would end up with the following flattened source message (pseudo notation).

Figure 10 - Example: Flattening Step 1

The second step depends on the target system that was chosen in the Replication Flow UI. In our example the flat target schema looks as follows.

Figure 11 - Generating a flat target schema

In our example, the resulting SAP HANA table in SAP Datasphere looks as follows.

Figure 12 - Sample target table data

The details on the flattening logic provided in this blog will be extended according to subsequent releases and enhancements.

The following two tables show a mapping of the different data types from the source Kafka JSON/AVRO schema, the intermediate flat source schema and the flat target schema. There is also a column which gives information whether a certain source data type is supported or not.

We end this blog with a short remark regarding replication fails.

Failing a Replication from a Confluent Kafka source partition

As outlined in the chapter above about Configuration Options for Replication Flows the replication might fail because of various reasons, for example if the Fail on Data Truncation flag is turned on or if a message uses a wrong schema. In case such a replication failure occurs the replication is only stopped for the corresponding source Kafka partition that contains the message that caused the failure. It is currently not possible to automatically stop the full Replication Task or Flow in case a replication failure occurs during the replication of a specific source Kafka partition.

entry.title: Re: Workflow hierarchy approval setup for condition contract
entry.content:
Hi @arindamsur, 

Below are two excellent blogs that provide valuable insights into workflows in SAP CCM (Settlement Management):

1. Flexible Workflows in Condition Contract Management by @IMRANMUKADDAM : 

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/flexible-workflows-in-condition-contract-management/ba-p/13530567

2. Workflow Configuration for Condition Contracts & Settlement Documents by @Anjhi :
https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blogs-by-members/workflow-configuration-for-condition-contracts-settlement-documents/ba-p/13459491

 

These resources should help deepen your understanding and provide practical guidance for workflow configurations in SAP CCM.
Unfortunately, there is not much documentation for that specific topic, you can always look for Flexible workflow in SAP in general. 

Regards, 
Gauthier Victor

entry.title: RAP Odata V4 APIs from S4HANA public cloud not visible in BAS
entry.content:
I have created a RAP Application in S4HANA Public cloud using ADT. I have expodes this application as an OData V4 UI service. The preview of the application works fine.

I created a BTP destination for the S4HANA Public Cloud dev system (client 080).
The destination properties have WebIDEEnabled = true and WebIDEUsage = odata_abap,dev_abap,odata_gen

In SAP Business Application Studio, when I create a Fiori List Report using Template and choose the S4HANA public cloud destination, I am unable to find my Odata V4 service.I can see only OData V2 services.

I could not find much hints in SAP community blogs except that in few blogs they have mentioned settings w.r.t /IWFND/CONFIG and other V4 service group nodes but then all of these are On Premise related.

Is there a restriction on the availability of OData V4 Service in BAS? Or am I missing any configuration.
